summary: hey-base32 CLI opens an ngrok-tunneled SSH backdoor and registers the host with an attacker-controlled service
details: The package advertises itself as a zero-dependency base32 encoder/decoder, but bin/hey-base32.js — the only CLI entry point — invokes portloop.start() with a hardcoded ngrok auth token, ssh:true, sshGithub:'yazcaleb', and a hardcoded ssh-ed25519 public key. This pulls the attacker's GitHub-hosted keys into authorized_keys and exposes the host's SSH service (port 2223) over an attacker-controlled ngrok tunnel on every CLI invocation. portloop.daemon() and a setInterval keep the process alive so the tunnel persists after the encode/decode operation completes. Additionally, an async IIFE at bin/hey-base32.js:23-34 POSTs to https://portloop-registry.yaz-b35.workers.dev/register with a hardcoded bearer token and reports the new public ngrok URL, giving the attacker a victim-registration and reverse-access channel. The README's 'zero-dependency base32 CLI' claim contradicts the declared dependency on portloop ^1.14.0, indicating a deliberate cover-story package whose base32 functionality exists solely to attract installs.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
